---
title: "Food allergy in a changing dietary landscape: A focus on the Asia Pacific region"
author: "Agnes Sze-yin Leung, Punchama Pacharn, Sirinoot Tangvalelerd, Sakura Sato, Elizabeth Pitt, Gary Wong et al."
year: 2024
doi: "10.1111/pai.14211"
journal: "Pediatric Allergy and Immunology"
volume: "35(8):e14211"
pmid: "DON'T KNOW"
access: "Open access — CC BY-NC 4.0. PDF accessed via institutional download (Wiley Online Library, dated 31/03/2026)."
source_quality: "Full text read from PDF."
---

# Food allergy in a changing dietary landscape: A focus on the Asia Pacific region

**Authors:** Agnes Sze-yin Leung (CUHK Hong Kong), Punchama Pacharn, Sirinoot Tangvalelerd, Sakura Sato, Elizabeth Pitt, Gary Wong et al.
**Year:** 2024
**DOI:** 10.1111/pai.14211
**Journal:** Pediatric Allergy and Immunology, 35(8):e14211

## Why this paper

Brought in for two reasons: (1) it reports the EuroPrevall-INCO children's data from the India sites (Mysore and Bengaluru) with full methodology; (2) it synthesises Asia Pacific food allergy patterns including regional allergen differences and the dietary shift hypothesis. It complements P02 (Mahesh 2023b) and P04 (Krishna 2020) which also cite EuroPrevall data — this paper gives more detail on the study design and the cross-site comparison.

## Extracted points — EuroPrevall-INCO data

> **Source quality note:** Full text read from PDF. All numbers below are from the paper. Text in quotation marks is taken directly. Table data in the paper was partially garbled in PDF extraction (table format); numbers are from the prose sections.

### EuroPrevall-INCO study design

1. The EuroPrevall-INCO study recruited "35,549 children aged 6 to 11 years from China (Hong Kong [HK], Guangzhou & Shaoguan), Russia (Tomsk), and India (Mysore & Bengaluru)." — [FULL TEXT, direct quote]

2. Definition of probable food allergy used: "reported symptoms and positive skin prick test (SPT) or specific IgE (sIgE) (≥0.7 kUA/L) to the specific food." — [FULL TEXT, direct quote]
   Note: This is the same "probable food allergy" definition as in P02.

3. India sites specifically: Mysore and Bengaluru, Karnataka state, South India. The Li et al. 2019 study (which reports the India data from EuroPrevall) is cited as reference 18.

### EuroPrevall-INCO prevalence comparison across sites

4. Prevalence of probable food allergy by site:
   - Hong Kong (HK): 1.5% — "a highly Westernized city"
   - Shaoguan (rural-ish China): 0.69%
   - Guangzhou (urban China): 0.21%
   - India (Mysore + Bengaluru): 0.14% — [FULL TEXT, from prose section]

5. "Despite a similar genetic background, the prevalence of probable food allergy was significantly higher in HK (1.5%), a highly Westernized city, compared to the two other Chinese centers." — [FULL TEXT, direct quote]
   "Children born in HK were four times more likely to develop food sensitization than those born in mainland China who migrated to HK." — [FULL TEXT, direct quote]
   For us: This is the migration/urbanisation effect quantified — 4× increase in sensitisation risk from same genetic stock in different environments. Directly supports the argument that India's current low prevalence may increase with urbanisation.

### India-specific allergen data

6. From the paper's allergen pattern analysis: India allergen data is not separately tabulated in the prose sections extracted. The paper notes that shrimp dominated in China and fish in Russia — DON'T KNOW what the paper says about India's dominant allergens specifically beyond what is in P02 (legumes, prawn, eggplant, milk, egg).

7. "In Vietnam, shellfish allergy was the major food allergen while peanut allergy was rare in both urban and rural areas of Vietnam (0.27% in Hue city and 0.31% in Tien Giang Province)." — [FULL TEXT, direct quote]
   For us: Peanut rarity is not unique to India — it is a broader Asian pattern. This contextualises India's 0.03% peanut allergy figure.

8. "It is unclear why peanuts are uncommon food allergens in Asia, while they are consistently the top food allergens and common anaphylaxis triggers in the West." — [FULL TEXT, direct quote]

### Regional allergen patterns (Asia Pacific)

9. Eggs: consistently reported as common in young children across Asia Pacific. — [FULL TEXT, general statement from paper]

10. Shellfish: predominates in older children across the region. — [FULL TEXT, general statement]

11. Shrimp dominant in China; fish dominant in Russia. — [FULL TEXT]

### Dietary shift and allergy trajectory argument

12. Paper's key message (from abstract/graphical abstract): "The rise in the prevalence of food allergies can largely be attributed to environmental factors, which are influenced by the adoption of Westernized lifestyles and diets." — [FULL TEXT, direct quote from key message box]

13. "The shift in the pattern of peanuts, tree nuts, wheat, and egg allergies calls for ongoing research and public health strategies to mitigate their impact." — [FULL TEXT, direct quote from key message box]

14. Rising trend in Asia (in contrast to peanut allergy rise in West): "wheat allergies" and "tree nut-induced anaphylaxis cases" in Japan, with tree nut anaphylaxis climbing from 6.0% to 15.3% between 2017 and 2019 in Aichi prefecture. — [FULL TEXT]
    For us: This is Japan-specific data, not India. The trend is noted for its trajectory argument — if India follows similar patterns with westernisation, wheat and tree nut may rise.

15. Migration effect (Australia data): "prevalence of food allergy is around 3-fold higher among children born in Australia to parents who migrated from Asia, compared to children of Australian-born parents. The parents themselves had a lower rate of allergy." — [FULL TEXT, direct quote]

## Gaps documented

- India-specific data is limited to Karnataka (South India) in this paper — no data from other Indian states
- DON'T KNOW: What the paper says about allergens specifically affecting Indian children beyond the EuroPrevall panel
- Mechanism of protection in low-prevalence settings is noted as incompletely understood: "Addressing this knowledge gap presents an opportunity to develop more effective prevention and treatment strategies." — [FULL TEXT, direct quote]

## What this paper does not address

- FSSAI regulatory framework: not covered
- North India or rural India food allergy patterns: not covered
- Indigenous Indian allergens (black gram, legumes beyond EuroPrevall panel): not discussed outside EuroPrevall panel
